metadata.title: Why Using AI More Made These Engineers Learn Less
metadata.note: Exclusions: no discussion of vibe-coding etymology, no AI policy debate, no coverage of the Kosmyna EEG study or other adjacent studies — one RCT is enough; no mention of IBM entry-level hiring

beats.name: Title
beats.narration_text: Two engineers. Same AI tool. Same problem. Same two hours. The test that follows: 46 points apart.

beats.name: ColdScene
beats.narration_text: Tuesday afternoon. A Python library neither of them has seen. Both open the same chat window and start typing.

props.headline: Tuesday afternoon. A Python library neither of them has seen. Both…

beats.scene_description: two laptop screens side by side, both showing a chat interface, editorial collage on aged newsprint
beats.image_prompt: B02, two open laptops side by side on a wood desk, both screens showing identical chat interfaces, soft editorial light, newsprint collage aesthetic, desaturated tones, flat print reproduction

beats.name: TheQuestion
beats.narration_text: Using AI should make you better at the task. Here is a controlled experiment where using it more made engineers understand it less. Why?

beats.name: TheTrial
beats.narration_text: Anthropic's 2026 randomized trial. Fifty-two junior engineers. One group used an AI assistant. The other wrote every line by hand. Same problem. Same time. Then: a comprehension quiz.

beats.name: TheGap
beats.narration_text: Hand-coding group: 67 percent. AI-assisted group: 50 percent. Seventeen points. Nearly two letter grades. One variable: who did the executing.

beats.name: InsideTheGroup
beats.narration_text: But inside the AI group, behavior split the outcome cleanly. Some engineers interrogated the model — reading every answer, asking why, pushing back. Others delegated — paste the problem, accept the output, move on.

beats.name: ThreeBarReveal
beats.narration_text: Interrogators scored 65 to 86 percent — matching the hand-coders. Delegators scored 24 to 39 percent. Same tool. Same task. Opposite result.

beats.name: TheMechanism
beats.narration_text: Execution and comprehension are different events. AI handles execution. Comprehension is built by the struggle to understand what was produced.

beats.name: TheImplication
beats.narration_text: Delegation skips that struggle. The output is perfect. The learning never happened. The variable was not the AI. It was whether the human kept checking.

beats.name: PriyaAndJames
beats.narration_text: Priya and James both use Claude to learn a Python library. Priya reads every line, asks why. James pastes prompts, accepts outputs. Two hours later: same code on screen. Post-quiz: Priya 71 percent, James 31 percent. Illustrative numbers — same mechanism.

beats.name: Endcard
beats.narration_text: The tool was identical. The checking was not. Let the machine type. Keep the checking for yourself.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the The Reallocation Engine series.
